competitors.label: <a>: The Anchor element
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN reference for the anchor element, hyperlink semantics, navigation behavior, accessibility, and security notes around target and rel.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on what the anchor element does, how href creates navigation, and what target, rel, and accessibility expectations apply.
theirGaps: The reference is broader and less explicit about the interview-grade distinction between links for navigation and buttons for in-page actions.

realWorldUseCases: The <a> (anchor) tag is the browser's real navigation primitive: it gives links semantics, accessibility, and SEO behavior, and it avoids common button-vs-link pitfalls in production UIs.
followUpConfusion: In production, the common mistake is treating anchors and buttons as interchangeable: <a> preserves link semantics, open-in-new-tab behavior, browser history, and SEO, while <button> is for in-page actions.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines links comprehensively, but it stays closer to the element reference than to one concrete production decision about navigation semantics versus button semantics.
actionableExamples: It includes direct anchor examples and security notes, so the example criterion stays competitive rather than flipping fully to us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more explicit about the follow-up confusion interviewers usually want: when a UI should be an anchor, when it should be a button, and how target or rel changes the decision.

actionableExamples: Keep the navigation-versus-button contrast near the opening so the page stays faster to scan than the full reference.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical anchor reference, but our page is more useful as an interview answer because it frames the anchor tag around the exact production mistake teams make most: using links and buttons interchangeably.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant because link semantics, navigation behavior, and anchor usage are core HTML interview topics.
theirStrengths: It is a strong HTML interview-prep source with broad coverage of common markup and accessibility questions.
theirGaps: The category page is not dedicated to anchor semantics, so it is less focused on one exact navigation-versus-button answer.

realWorldUseCases: The <a> (anchor) tag is the browser's real navigation primitive: it gives links semantics, accessibility, and SEO behavior, and it avoids common button-vs-link pitfalls in production UIs.
followUpConfusion: In production, the common mistake is treating anchors and buttons as interchangeable: <a> preserves link semantics, open-in-new-tab behavior, browser history, and SEO, while <button> is for in-page actions.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd is relevant for HTML prep, but the category page is less explicit about the navigation semantics and production trade-offs that make anchors different from buttons.
actionableExamples: It remains a strong worked-answer bank, so the example criterion stays tied rather than becoming an automatic win for us.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the likely follow-up: fake links, target blank security, and why descriptive link text matters.

actionableExamples: Keep the security and accessibility essentials visible so the answer stays operational, not only conceptual.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is broad HTML prep, but our page is more specific about the semantic and production distinctions that make a strong anchor-tag answer.

competitors.label: Links
competitors.selectionReason: web.dev has a direct links module that covers the anchor element, href, navigation targets, and real browser behavior around hyperlinking.
theirStrengths: It is strong on link fundamentals, href usage, and the broader HTML navigation model.
theirGaps: It is broader than the exact trivia question because it covers more of the link ecosystem than only the purpose of the anchor tag.

realWorldUseCases: The <a> (anchor) tag is the browser's real navigation primitive: it gives links semantics, accessibility, and SEO behavior, and it avoids common button-vs-link pitfalls in production UIs.
followUpConfusion: In production, the common mistake is treating anchors and buttons as interchangeable: <a> preserves link semantics, open-in-new-tab behavior, browser history, and SEO, while <button> is for in-page actions.

realWorldUseCases: web.dev is grounded in practical hyperlink behavior, but our page packages the anchor question more directly around the production decision between navigation and in-page action.
actionableExamples: The tutorial contains solid link examples and href cases, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our page is clearer on the next debugging questions around fake links, security attributes, and accessible link text.

actionableExamples: Keep the practical scenario in the dashboard sidebar so the page retains one concrete UI decision point.

competitors.winReason: web.dev is strong on links generally, but our page is better as a compact interview answer because it turns the anchor tag into a concrete navigation-semantics and production-debugging choice.
